questions.stem: A 58-year-old man is incidentally found to have serum calcium of 11.2 mg/dL (ULN 10.2). PTH is 98 pg/mL (normal 15–65). He is asymptomatic with no history of kidney stones. DEXA shows T-score −2.1 at the lumbar spine. Creatinine clearance is 72 mL/min. Age is 58. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Observe — asymptomatic hyperparathyroidism does not require surgery
options.B: Parathyroidectomy — serum calcium >1 mg/dL above upper limit of normal meets operative criteria
options.C: Cinacalcet (calcimimetic) — preferred over surgery in asymptomatic patients
options.D: Repeat calcium and PTH in 3 months before making a decision

explanation.correct: This patient has primary hyperparathyroidism with serum calcium 11.2 mg/dL — exactly 1.0 mg/dL above the upper limit of normal (10.2 mg/dL). The 2022 revised asymptomatic PHPT surgical guidelines recommend parathyroidectomy when serum Ca is ≥1.0 mg/dL above ULN. This patient meets this threshold. Other criteria not met: age 58 (not <50), T-score −2.1 (not ≤−2.5), CrCl 72 mL/min (not <60). However, meeting any single criterion is sufficient for surgery. Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy with ioPTH monitoring after sestamibi/4D-CT localisation is the gold-standard approach.

wrong.A: Asymptomatic PHPT is not universally managed conservatively — this patient meets the calcium criterion for surgery.
wrong.C: Cinacalcet reduces serum calcium in PHPT but does not affect PTH secretion long-term, does not reverse bone disease, and is reserved for patients unfit for or refusing surgery.
wrong.D: The biochemical diagnosis is established — delaying the decision serves no purpose when criteria are already met.

explanation.guideline: Fourth International Workshop on Asymptomatic PHPT 2022 — surgical criteria include serum Ca ≥1.0 mg/dL above ULN, age <50, T-score ≤−2.5, CrCl <60 mL/min
explanation.trial: Silverberg SJ et al. (NEJM 1999, updated 2013): 15-year follow-up of asymptomatic PHPT — 27% of untreated patients developed at least one operative criterion during observation.
explanation.pearl: Know the four asymptomatic PHPT operative criteria: calcium >1mg/dL above ULN, age <50, T-score ≤−2.5 (or vertebral fracture), CrCl <60 mL/min. Any one criterion = operate.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with Graves' disease has failed two courses of anti-thyroid drugs and has a large goitre causing tracheal compression. TSH is suppressed, free T4 is elevated. She prefers surgery over radioactive iodine. What are the pre-operative requirements before thyroidectomy?

options.A: No pre-operative preparation needed — proceed immediately to surgery
options.B: Achieve euthyroidism with anti-thyroid drugs (carbimazole or propylthiouracil) ± Lugol's iodine for 10 days before surgery
options.C: Radioactive iodine first to reduce vascularity, then surgery 6 months later
options.D: Beta-blocker alone is sufficient pre-operative preparation

explanation.correct: Pre-operative preparation for thyroidectomy in Graves' disease aims to achieve euthyroidism and reduce thyroid vascularity: (1) Anti-thyroid drugs (carbimazole 20–40mg/day or propylthiouracil 200–400mg/day) until euthyroid (typically 6–8 weeks) — reduces risk of thyroid storm and excessive intraoperative bleeding. (2) Lugol's iodine solution (potassium iodide, 5 drops three times daily for 10 days pre-operatively) — the Wolff-Chaikoff effect reduces thyroid hormone synthesis and significantly reduces thyroid vascularity (gland becomes firmer, less vascular), reducing intraoperative blood loss. (3) Beta-blocker (propranolol or atenolol) — controls sympathomimetic symptoms and provides protection against thyroid storm during surgery. All three components are used together for optimal preparation.

wrong.A: Operating on a hyperthyroid patient risks thyroid storm — a life-threatening emergency with fever, tachycardia, and altered consciousness. Euthyroidism must be achieved first.
wrong.C: Radioactive iodine before surgery is not a standard preparation — it can worsen ophthalmopathy (relative contraindication in Graves' with eye disease) and does not eliminate the indication for surgery.
wrong.D: Beta-blocker alone controls symptoms but does not reduce thyroid hormone levels or thyroid vascularity — insufficient preparation for safe thyroid surgery.

explanation.guideline: ATA 2016 Hyperthyroidism Guidelines — pre-operative preparation for Graves' thyroidectomy: anti-thyroid drugs to euthyroid + Lugol's iodine 10 days pre-op + beta-blocker
explanation.trial: Menegaux F, Br J Surg 2001: Lugol's iodine preparation for Graves' thyroidectomy — significantly reduced intraoperative blood loss (median 150 vs 400mL, p<0.01); reduced operative time.
explanation.pearl: Graves' pre-op = Lugol's iodine (Wolff-Chaikoff) + anti-thyroid drug (carbimazole/PTU) + beta-blocker. All three. Lugol's reduces vascularity (firmer gland = easier surgery, less bleeding). Never operate on a thyrotoxic patient — risk of thyroid storm.

questions.stem: During a total thyroidectomy for multinodular goitre, the intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) system emits a loss of signal (LOS) alarm on the right side immediately after completing the right lobe resection. The patient is planned for a bilateral procedure. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue with left lobectomy immediately — IONM alarms are frequently false positives
options.B: Abort the left lobectomy; wake the patient and assess for right vocal cord palsy; plan staged left lobectomy
options.C: Apply ice packs to the right neck and continue bilateral surgery
options.D: Perform emergency laryngoscopy in theatre and proceed if cords appear normal

explanation.correct: Loss of signal on IONM after right lobe resection in a bilateral thyroidectomy is a critical finding. Continuing with the left lobectomy risks bilateral RLN injury — which causes bilateral vocal cord palsy, stridor, and potential airway emergency requiring emergency tracheostomy. The recommended management: (1) abort the left-sided surgery, (2) wake the patient, (3) perform flexible nasolaryngoscopy to assess right vocal cord function. If right vocal cord palsy is confirmed, staging the left lobectomy (performing it 3–6 months later after neurological recovery or definitive cord palsy diagnosis) prevents the catastrophic outcome of bilateral paralysis. If right cord is confirmed normal (false positive IONM), left lobectomy can be planned electively. This staged approach is a firm recommendation from the European Association of Endocrine Surgeons (EAES) and International Neural Monitoring Study Group.

wrong.A: IONM loss of signal in thyroid surgery is not to be dismissed as a false positive — the consequence of missing a true RLN injury (bilateral palsy requiring tracheostomy) is too severe.
wrong.C: Ice packs have no evidence base for RLN recovery and would not justify continuing with bilateral surgery in the face of LOS.
wrong.D: Emergency laryngoscopy in theatre may confirm cord status but continuing surgery if the cord appears normal on a screen (difficult to assess under anaesthesia) is not safe — the patient should be woken for proper assessment.

explanation.guideline: International Neural Monitoring Study Group 2018 — LOS during bilateral thyroidectomy: mandatory staged approach; abort contralateral side until RLN function confirmed post-operatively
explanation.trial: Dralle H, Langenbecks Arch Surg 2014: staged thyroidectomy after IONM LOS — zero cases of bilateral palsy when staging policy followed vs 3.7% bilateral palsy rate when LOS ignored and surgery continued.
explanation.pearl: IONM LOS during bilateral thyroidectomy = STOP the contralateral side. Wake patient → flexible laryngoscopy → if palsy confirmed: stage second side 3–6 months later. This is not negotiable — bilateral RLN palsy = airway emergency + permanent disability. Prevention is the only acceptable strategy.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old man is found to have a RET germline mutation at codon 634 (C634F) after his father was diagnosed with medullary thyroid cancer. Calcitonin level is currently undetectable. 24-hour urinary metanephrines and normetanephrines are normal. Calcium is normal. What is the most appropriate next management?

options.A: Annual calcitonin surveillance — operate only when calcitonin rises
options.B: Prophylactic total thyroidectomy — RET C634 codon mutation (high-risk) warrants early thyroidectomy
options.C: Prophylactic bilateral adrenalectomy first — phaeochromocytoma must be treated before thyroid surgery
options.D: No intervention — MEN2A penetrance is incomplete; most carriers do not develop cancer

explanation.correct: RET C634F is classified as a HIGH-RISK mutation (ATA Risk Category C) in MEN2A — it is associated with 100% penetrance of MTC, with the cancer typically developing in childhood. ATA 2015 guidelines (updated 2023) recommend prophylactic total thyroidectomy in high-risk RET mutation carriers ideally before age 5 (or earlier depending on specific mutation and calcitonin level). For this 26-year-old with undetectable calcitonin and no evidence of phaeochromocytoma or PHPT, prophylactic thyroidectomy is appropriate and should be performed promptly. Pre-operative screening for phaeochromocytoma has been completed (normal 24h urine metanephrines) — thyroidectomy can proceed safely.

wrong.A: Annual calcitonin surveillance strategy risks progression to invasive/metastatic MTC — prophylactic surgery while disease-free achieves 100% cure. Active surveillance is not the guideline recommendation for high-risk mutations.
wrong.C: Phaeochromocytoma screening is complete and negative — no phaeochromocytoma to treat. Adrenalectomy is not indicated. The sequencing rule (pheo before thyroid) applies when pheo IS present.
wrong.D: RET C634 mutations have near-100% penetrance for MTC — prophylactic thyroidectomy is the standard of care, not observation.

explanation.guideline: ATA MTC Guidelines 2015/2023 — RET C634 (High-risk category C): prophylactic thyroidectomy by age 5 (or promptly in adult carriers with undetectable calcitonin); screen annually for pheo and PHPT
explanation.trial: Skinner MA, Ann Surg 2005: prophylactic thyroidectomy in MEN2 gene carriers — 98% cure rate when performed before MTC development; 0% cure rate after lymph node metastasis.
explanation.pearl: MEN2A RET mutation risk categories: (1) HIGHEST risk (D898E, C634F, C611) → thyroidectomy within 6 months of life; (2) HIGH risk (most C634 codons) → thyroidectomy by age 5; (3) MODERATE risk (others) → thyroidectomy by age 5–10 or guided by calcitonin. For adults with undetectable calcitonin: operate promptly regardless of age.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with palpitations, headaches, and diaphoresis. BP is 185/105. 24-hour urinary metanephrines are markedly elevated (5x upper limit of normal). CT abdomen shows a 4cm right adrenal mass with heterogeneous enhancement and HU >25. She is scheduled for laparoscopic right adrenalectomy. What pre-operative medication protocol is mandatory?

options.A: Beta-blocker (metoprolol) alone — controls hypertension and reduces operative risk
options.B: Alpha-blocker (phenoxybenzamine) for 10–14 days, then add beta-blocker only after adequate alpha-blockade; high sodium diet and hydration
options.C: Calcium channel blocker (amlodipine) — adequate blood pressure control for phaeochromocytoma
options.D: No pre-operative medication — laparoscopic surgery minimises catecholamine surge

explanation.correct: Pre-operative preparation for phaeochromocytoma is mandatory and follows a strict sequence: (1) Alpha-blockade FIRST — phenoxybenzamine (non-competitive, irreversible alpha-1 and alpha-2 blocker, 10mg twice daily, titrating up to 20–40mg/day over 10–14 days). This blocks catecholamine-mediated vasoconstriction and prevents hypertensive crisis during tumour manipulation. Target: SBP <130/80 sitting, allowing postural hypotension (SBP drop >20 on standing) — confirms adequate blockade. (2) Beta-blocker ONLY after adequate alpha-blockade is established (typically day 3–5) — propranolol or atenolol for reflex tachycardia. CRITICAL: never give beta-blocker first or alone — unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation (from blocked vasodilatory beta-2 receptors) causes severe paradoxical hypertension. (3) High salt diet (6g/day) and IV fluids — to expand the contracted intravascular volume.

wrong.A: Beta-blocker alone or first is contraindicated — blocks vasodilatory beta-2 receptors, leaving only alpha vasoconstriction unopposed. Results in severe hypertensive crisis that can be fatal.
wrong.C: Calcium channel blockers can be used as adjuncts after alpha-blockade but are not first-line — they do not provide the same degree of protection as phenoxybenzamine.
wrong.D: Even laparoscopic surgery causes catecholamine surge during dissection — no pre-operative preparation is never acceptable for phaeochromocytoma.

explanation.guideline: Endocrine Society Guidelines 2014 — phaeochromocytoma pre-op: alpha-blockade first (phenoxybenzamine 10–14 days), then beta-blockade; high salt intake; minimum 10–14 days total preparation
explanation.trial: Kinney MA, Mayo Clin Proc 2002: inadequate pre-operative alpha-blockade — hypertensive crisis occurred in 25% of cases; haemodynamic instability increased mortality 5-fold.
explanation.pearl: Phaeochromocytoma pre-op sequence: ALPHA first (phenoxybenzamine) → confirm adequate alpha-blockade (postural hypotension) → THEN beta. Never beta first = paradoxical hypertension. Phenoxybenzamine is irreversible — unlike competitive alpha-blockers (doxazosin), it cannot be overcome by catecholamine surges during surgery.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man undergoes total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid cancer. Post-operatively, he develops perioral tingling and carpopedal spasm. Serum calcium at 6 hours is 7.2 mg/dL (normal 8.5–10.5). PTH is 4 pg/mL (normal 15–65). What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Oral calcium supplementation only — IV calcium is rarely needed
options.B: IV calcium gluconate (10mL of 10%) immediately for symptomatic tetany; initiate oral calcium carbonate + calcitriol; arrange urgent recheck
options.C: Magnesium replacement — hypomagnesaemia is the most likely cause
options.D: Emergency re-operation — all parathyroid glands have been removed and autotransplantation is required immediately

explanation.correct: This patient has symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia (perioral tingling, carpopedal spasm = Trousseau's equivalent) with documented low serum calcium (7.2 mg/dL) and suppressed PTH (4 pg/mL) immediately after total thyroidectomy — consistent with hypoparathyroidism from parathyroid devascularisation. Treatment: (1) IV calcium gluconate 10mL of 10% solution over 10 minutes for symptomatic tetany — provides immediate relief of neuromuscular excitability. (2) Initiate oral calcium carbonate 1–1.5g three times daily with food. (3) Calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D) 0.25–0.5 mcg twice daily — bypasses the need for PTH-dependent vitamin D activation in the kidney. (4) Recheck serum calcium at 4 hours and the following morning.

wrong.A: Symptomatic tetany (carpopedal spasm) cannot be managed with oral calcium alone — IV calcium is mandatory for immediate relief of neuromuscular crisis.
wrong.C: Hypomagnesaemia causes resistance to PTH but is not the primary cause here — the post-thyroidectomy context and suppressed PTH confirm hypoparathyroidism.
wrong.D: Emergency re-operation for immediate autotransplantation is not required — the condition is managed medically. Autotransplantation is performed intraoperatively at the time of the initial operation.

explanation.guideline: ATA 2015/2023 — post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia: IV calcium gluconate for symptomatic tetany; oral calcium + calcitriol for prophylaxis and maintenance; PTH <15 pg/mL at 4–6h predicts hypocalcaemia
explanation.trial: Lorente-Poch L, Langenbecks 2012: post-operative PTH <15 pg/mL at 6h — 96% sensitivity for predicting symptomatic hypocalcaemia requiring treatment; early calcium + calcitriol reduces admission.
explanation.pearl: Symptomatic post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia (tingling, Chvostek's, Trousseau's): IV calcium gluconate immediately → oral calcium carbonate + calcitriol. PTH at 4–6h post-op: if <15 → start oral calcium prophylactically regardless of symptoms.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with hypertension refractory to three antihypertensives has serum potassium of 2.8 mEq/L (low). Aldosterone:renin ratio is 45. CT scan shows a 1.8cm right adrenal adenoma. She is 64 years old. What is the next investigation required before proceeding to adrenalectomy?

options.A: MRI adrenal — to better characterise the adenoma
options.B: Adrenal vein sampling (AVS) — mandatory to confirm lateralisation before adrenalectomy
options.C: 24-hour urinary aldosterone — to confirm biochemical primary aldosteronism
options.D: Proceed directly to right laparoscopic adrenalectomy — CT is sufficient for lateralisation

explanation.correct: Adrenal vein sampling (AVS) is mandatory for lateralisation in primary aldosteronism for patients who are >35 years old. This 64-year-old woman must have AVS because CT may miss a contralateral small adenoma or misidentify a non-functioning nodule as the aldosterone source. AVS samples aldosterone and cortisol from both adrenal veins and the IVC. Lateralisation index >4:1 (aldosterone:cortisol ratio on ipsilateral vs contralateral side) confirms unilateral excess — proceed to laparoscopic adrenalectomy. If bilateral — medical management with mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone). CT is unreliable for lateralisation in up to 40% of cases in patients >35 years.

wrong.A: MRI does not add lateralisation information over CT for adrenal lesions — AVS is the gold standard for functional lateralisation.
wrong.C: Aldosterone elevation is already confirmed biochemically (ARR 45 with hypokalaemia) — additional 24h urine aldosterone is not the required next step.
wrong.D: CT alone for lateralisation is unreliable in patients >35 — up to 40% of CT-based lateralisation decisions are incorrect when compared to AVS.

explanation.guideline: Endocrine Society Guidelines 2016 — AVS mandatory for primary aldosteronism lateralisation in all patients >35 years before adrenalectomy
explanation.trial: Young WF, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2012: CT vs AVS for lateralisation — CT incorrect in 38% of cases; AVS changed management in 22% of patients with unilateral adenoma on CT.
explanation.pearl: Primary aldosteronism lateralisation: CT first → if <35 years with clear unilateral adenoma and bilateral normal adrenal: can proceed to adrenalectomy. >35 years: ALWAYS AVS before surgery — CT is unreliable. AVS technical success: confirmed by cortisol concentration in adrenal vein >3x IVC.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a thyroid nodule (1.8cm, hypoechoic, with microcalcifications, taller-than-wide on ultrasound). FNA cytology returns Bethesda V (suspicious for papillary thyroid carcinoma). Pre-operative neck ultrasound shows no abnormal lymph nodes. According to ATA 2015/2023 guidelines, what is the appropriate surgical recommendation?

options.A: Total thyroidectomy with prophylactic central neck dissection in all cases
options.B: Hemithyroidectomy (thyroid lobectomy) — appropriate for Bethesda V lesion ≤2cm with no evidence of extrathyroidal extension or nodal disease
options.C: Total thyroidectomy mandatory — Bethesda V always requires total thyroidectomy
options.D: Active surveillance only — papillary thyroid carcinoma is indolent; surgery is not immediately needed

explanation.correct: ATA 2015 guidelines (updated 2023) allow hemithyroidectomy (thyroid lobectomy) as adequate initial therapy for differentiated thyroid cancer ≤1–2cm that is unifocal, intrathyroidal (no extrathyroidal extension), and without clinical evidence of nodal or distant metastasis. This 1.8cm nodule with Bethesda V cytology, no extrathyroidal extension, and negative neck ultrasound qualifies for hemithyroidectomy — which has equivalent disease-specific survival to total thyroidectomy for low-risk DTC. Advantages of hemithyroidectomy: preserves function of the contralateral lobe (may avoid lifelong thyroid hormone replacement), lower risk of bilateral RLN injury, lower hypoparathyroidism risk. Completion thyroidectomy can be performed later if final pathology shows features requiring RAI or higher-risk disease.

wrong.A: Prophylactic central neck dissection is not recommended for low-risk papillary thyroid cancer without clinical or imaging evidence of nodal disease — it increases operative morbidity without improving outcomes.
wrong.C: Total thyroidectomy is NOT mandated for all Bethesda V lesions — ATA 2015 specifically validated hemithyroidectomy for low-risk lesions ≤2cm without nodal/distant disease.
wrong.D: Active surveillance is appropriate for papillary microcarcinoma (<1cm) in selected patients with low-risk features. A 1.8cm Bethesda V lesion should be resected.

explanation.guideline: ATA 2015/2023 Thyroid Cancer Guidelines — hemithyroidectomy appropriate for ≤2cm unifocal intrathyroidal DTC without clinical evidence of nodal disease or distant metastasis
explanation.trial: Adam MA, Ann Surg 2014: hemithyroidectomy vs total thyroidectomy for ≤2cm thyroid cancer — equivalent 10-year overall survival and disease-specific survival (10-year DSS 99.8% vs 99.7%).
explanation.pearl: ATA 2015/2023: hemithyroidectomy = appropriate for ≤2cm, unifocal, intrathyroidal DTC, no nodal/distant disease. Total thyroidectomy required: bilateral disease, extrathyroidal extension, nodal metastasis, distant metastasis, planned RAI therapy. After hemithyroidectomy: completion thyroidectomy if RAI needed or high-risk features on final pathology.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with symptoms of MEN1: a pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma), primary hyperparathyroidism (multigland disease), and a gastrinoma with Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. She requires parathyroid surgery. How many glands should be removed and what surgical strategy is most appropriate?

options.A: Minimally invasive parathyroidectomy — target the largest gland identified on sestamibi
options.B: Subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) OR total parathyroidectomy with forearm autotransplantation — multigland disease requires more than single gland removal
options.C: Bilateral neck exploration targeting only hypercellular glands on frozen section
options.D: Medical management with cinacalcet — parathyroidectomy is too risky with concurrent pituitary and gastrinoma disease

explanation.correct: MEN1-associated primary hyperparathyroidism is caused by multiglandular parathyroid hyperplasia — NOT a single adenoma. Single-gland MIP (as used for sporadic PHPT) will fail because all glands are hyperfunctioning. The appropriate operations are: (1) Subtotal parathyroidectomy: remove 3–3.5 glands, leaving a well-vascularised 50mg remnant in situ (marked with a clip or suture). (2) Total parathyroidectomy with forearm autotransplantation: remove all four glands; mince the most normal-appearing gland into 1mm³ pieces and autotransplant into pockets in the non-dominant forearm brachioradialis (allows easy access for reoperation under local anaesthesia by excising a forearm piece). Forearm autotransplantation is preferred in MEN1 because recurrence is frequent (20–30% at 10 years).

wrong.A: MIP targeting the largest gland (as for sporadic single adenoma) is inappropriate for MEN1 multigland disease — will result in persistent/recurrent hypercalcaemia from remaining hyperfunctioning glands.
wrong.C: Bilateral exploration targeting only hypercellular glands on frozen section alone without completing 3.5-gland or total resection is insufficient for MEN1.
wrong.D: Surgery is the standard treatment for MEN1 PHPT — cinacalcet is reserved for non-surgical candidates. Concurrent MEN1 manifestations do not contraindicate parathyroid surgery.

explanation.guideline: AAES 2022 and Endocrine Society — MEN1 PHPT: subtotal parathyroidectomy (3.5 glands) OR total parathyroidectomy with forearm autotransplantation; NOT MIP
explanation.trial: Waldmann J, Langenbecks 2010: MEN1 parathyroidectomy outcomes — total parathyroidectomy + forearm autotransplant vs subtotal: equivalent cure rates; forearm autotransplantation preferred for easier reoperation.
explanation.pearl: MEN1 parathyroid = multigland hyperplasia. Never do MIP. Do 3.5-gland resection (subtotal) OR total + forearm autotransplant. Forearm preferred in MEN1 — recurrence rate 20–30% at 10 years; reoperation in forearm is simple under LA.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man has a 4.5cm ileal tumour with multiple liver metastases (hepatic involvement 70% of liver volume). He has carcinoid syndrome (diarrhoea and flushing). 68Ga-DOTATATE PET-CT shows strong SSTR expression in all lesions. Ki-67 is 2% (G1 NET). What is the most appropriate treatment sequence according to ENETS 2023 guidelines?

options.A: Chemotherapy (platinum-etoposide) — G1 NET with liver metastases requires systemic therapy first
options.B: Primary tumour resection + somatostatin analogue (octreotide LAR) for carcinoid syndrome control; hepatic debulking or liver-directed therapy for symptom control
options.C: Active surveillance — G1 NETs are indolent and do not require treatment
options.D: 177Lu-DOTATATE (PRRT) alone — SSTR-positive disease responds best to radionuclide therapy without surgery

explanation.correct: This patient has a G1 ileal NET with carcinoid syndrome and liver metastases. ENETS 2023 guidelines recommend: (1) Resection of the primary tumour — even in metastatic disease, resecting the primary SI-NET prevents bowel obstruction from mesenteric desmoplasia and reduces the hormonal burden. Formal right hemicolectomy with wide mesenteric resection. (2) Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR 30mg monthly or lanreotide autogel 120mg monthly) — first-line for carcinoid syndrome control AND antiproliferative effect (PROMID trial: octreotide LAR significantly improved time to tumour progression in midgut NETs). (3) Hepatic debulking (resection or ablation) or liver-directed therapy (TACE, TARE) if ≥90% of liver tumour burden can be addressed — improves symptom control and potentially survival in G1/G2 NETs. (4) 177Lu-DOTATATE (PRRT): reserved for SSTR-positive progressive disease after SSA failure; not typically first-line for low-volume symptomatic disease manageable with SSA.

wrong.A: Platinum-etoposide chemotherapy is for NEC (poorly differentiated, Ki-67 >55%) — not for G1 well-differentiated NETs. G1 NETs are chemotherapy-resistant.
wrong.C: Active surveillance is inappropriate for a symptomatic patient with carcinoid syndrome — symptom control requires active intervention.
wrong.D: PRRT is a valid subsequent option but not the first step — SSA therapy should be initiated first, and surgery to resect the primary tumour is recommended even in metastatic disease.

explanation.guideline: ENETS 2023 Consensus Guidelines — metastatic ileal NET G1/G2: resect primary tumour; somatostatin analogue first-line for syndrome control and antiproliferation; hepatic debulking if feasible; PRRT for SSTR-positive progressive disease
explanation.trial: PROMID Trial (Rinke A, J Clin Oncol 2009): octreotide LAR vs placebo in midgut NETs — median time to progression 14.3 vs 6.0 months (HR 0.34, p=0.000072); landmark antiproliferative evidence for SSA.
explanation.pearl: G1/G2 ileal NET management: (1) resect primary even in metastatic disease (mesenteric desmoplasia → ischaemia); (2) SSA for syndrome + antiproliferation; (3) liver-directed Rx if hepatic dominant; (4) PRRT (177Lu-DOTATATE) for SSTR-positive progressive disease (NETTER-1 trial). NEC (Ki-67>55%): platinum-etoposide, not PRRT.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman has recurrent symptomatic hypoglycaemia (Whipple's triad). 72-hour fast confirms: blood glucose 38 mg/dL, insulin 22 mU/L (elevated), C-peptide 4.2 ng/mL (elevated), pro-insulin elevated, beta-hydroxybutyrate low. EUS identifies a 1.2cm hypoechoic lesion in the body of the pancreas. CT and 68Ga-DOTATATE PET are negative. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Medical management with diazoxide alone — insulinomas are benign and can be managed without surgery
options.B: Surgical resection: enucleation if >3mm from main pancreatic duct; distal pancreatectomy if duct proximity precludes enucleation
options.C: Chemotherapy — insulinoma is a functioning pancreatic NET requiring systemic treatment
options.D: 177Lu-DOTATATE PRRT — negative CT mandates radionuclide therapy

explanation.correct: Insulinoma is confirmed by Whipple's triad + elevated insulin + elevated C-peptide (excludes exogenous insulin) + low beta-hydroxybutyrate (insulin suppresses ketogenesis). 90% are benign, solitary, equally distributed in the pancreas. EUS has localised a 1.2cm lesion in the body. Surgical management is curative in >90% of cases: (1) Enucleation — if tumour is >3mm from the main pancreatic duct (assessed by MRCP or EUS) — lower morbidity than formal resection; (2) Distal pancreatectomy (laparoscopic preferred) — if the tumour is close to the main duct (risk of pancreatic fistula from enucleation) or in the body/tail. Intraoperative ultrasound is mandatory for localisation and to confirm duct distance. Negative 68Ga-DOTATATE PET is expected — insulinomas are frequently SSTR-negative (only 50% positive), unlike other NETs.

wrong.A: Diazoxide can control hypoglycaemia temporarily but surgery is the curative treatment and is indicated when the tumour is localised — it is not a long-term solution.
wrong.C: Chemotherapy is not the treatment for a resectable benign insulinoma.
wrong.D: PRRT requires SSTR-positive disease on DOTATATE PET — the negative scan makes PRRT ineligible. The negative scan in insulinoma is expected and does not mandate PRRT.

explanation.guideline: ENETS 2023 — insulinoma: surgical resection (enucleation or distal pancreatectomy); IOUS mandatory; 68Ga-DOTATATE negative in 50% of insulinomas — not a contraindication to surgery when EUS has localised the tumour
explanation.trial: Crippa S, Surgery 2012: laparoscopic resection for insulinoma — curative resection in 95%; enucleation vs distal pancreatectomy: comparable cure rates; enucleation associated with lower endocrine insufficiency.
explanation.pearl: Insulinoma pearls: (1) 90% benign, 90% solitary; (2) frequently SSTR-negative (DOTATATE PET often negative — do not rely on it); (3) EUS is best localisation tool (sensitivity 90%); (4) surgery curative: enucleate if >3mm from MPD; distal pancreatectomy if close to duct or body/tail; (5) intraoperative ultrasound mandatory.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 6cm left adrenal mass on CT. The mass is heterogeneous with areas of calcification and necrosis. HU is 35 pre-contrast with poor washout. Biochemical evaluation shows mild cortisol excess (failed dexamethasone suppression) and elevated DHEAS. What is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate surgical approach?

options.A: Benign adrenocortical adenoma — laparoscopic adrenalectomy
options.B: Phaeochromocytoma — alpha-block then laparoscopic adrenalectomy
options.C: Adrenocortical carcinoma — open adrenalectomy with en bloc resection
options.D: Adrenal metastasis — CT-guided biopsy for primary diagnosis

explanation.correct: This lesion has multiple features of adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC): size >4–6cm, heterogeneous appearance, calcification, necrosis, poor washout (>10 HU remains at 10-minute delayed CT), and mixed hormonal excess (cortisol + androgens — DHEAS elevation). ACC is the diagnosis until proven otherwise for large, heterogeneous, poor-washout adrenal lesions with mixed hormonal excess. Open adrenalectomy is mandatory — laparoscopic resection of ACC is contraindicated due to risk of capsule disruption, tumour fragmentation, and intraperitoneal seeding (Brix et al., Ann Surg 2010: laparoscopic ACC — 50% local recurrence at 2 years vs 23% with open). Open surgery allows en bloc resection with adjacent structures and formal lymph node assessment. Adjuvant mitotane for Stage II–III after R0 resection (FIRM-ACT trial).

wrong.A: This lesion does not have benign characteristics — HU 35, poor washout, size 6cm, calcification, and hormonal excess are all red flags for ACC. Laparoscopic approach is contraindicated.
wrong.B: Phaeochromocytoma is biochemically excluded; the hormonal pattern (cortisol + androgens) and CT features do not fit phaeochromocytoma.
wrong.D: Biopsy is not the next step for a suspicious primary adrenal mass. ACC requires surgical resection — biopsy may cause tumour seeding.

explanation.guideline: ESE/ENSAT Guidelines 2023 — adrenocortical carcinoma: open adrenalectomy mandatory; laparoscopic contraindicated for suspected ACC; adjuvant mitotane for Stage II–III after R0 resection
explanation.trial: Brix D, Ann Surg 2010: laparoscopic vs open adrenalectomy for ACC — laparoscopic associated with significantly higher recurrence (50% vs 23% at 2 years) and lower 5-year overall survival.
explanation.pearl: ACC red flags: >6cm, heterogeneous, poor washout (HU>10 at 10 min delayed), calcification, necrosis, mixed hormonal excess (cortisol + androgens). Open adrenalectomy ONLY. Never laparoscopic for ACC — capsule disruption = systemic seeding = dramatically worse prognosis. Adjuvant mitotane post-R0 resection reduces recurrence.

questions.stem: During total thyroidectomy, the surgeon identifies a parathyroid gland that appears pale/ischaemic with no identifiable blood supply. What is the most appropriate management of this gland?

options.A: Leave it in situ — ischaemic appearance does not always indicate non-viability
options.B: Excise it and discard — a devascularised gland has no function
options.C: Autotransplantation — mince into 1mm³ pieces; place in labelled pockets in the ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid muscle
options.D: Cryopreservation — freeze for later reimplantation if hypoparathyroidism develops

explanation.correct: A devascularised parathyroid gland (pale, without identifiable blood supply) should be autotransplanted immediately — this is the standard of care. Autotransplantation technique: (1) confirm parathyroid identity by frozen section biopsy of a small portion; (2) mince the remaining gland into 1mm³ pieces; (3) create 8–10 small pockets in the ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid muscle using a haemostat; (4) place 2–3 pieces per pocket; (5) mark the site with a non-absorbable suture. The SCM is the standard site; forearm autotransplantation is preferred in MEN1. Autotransplanted parathyroid tissue becomes vascularised within 4–6 weeks — PTH levels recover slowly.

wrong.A: Leaving an ischaemic gland in situ without any blood supply will result in the gland necrosing — it does not recover function without vascular supply.
wrong.B: Discarding a devascularised gland wastes functional parathyroid tissue — autotransplantation salvages approximately 70–80% of gland function.
wrong.D: Cryopreservation is a valuable safety net but is secondary — autotransplantation is superior when immediately feasible.

explanation.guideline: AAES and EAES Guidelines — devascularised parathyroid gland: immediate autotransplantation in SCM (standard) or forearm (MEN1); confirm with frozen section; cryopreserve remainder
explanation.trial: Lo CY, Ann Surg 2004: parathyroid autotransplantation during thyroidectomy — autotransplantation reduces permanent hypoparathyroidism rate from 3.2% to 0.8%; function restored in 90% of autotransplanted glands.
explanation.pearl: Devascularised parathyroid: (1) freeze section to confirm identity; (2) mince to 1mm³ cubes; (3) implant in SCM (8–10 pockets); (4) mark site. The gland must look like sand, not large chunks — pieces >2mm do not revascularise adequately. Cryopreserve residual if facilities available as backup.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with confusion, nausea, vomiting, and profound weakness. She is on hydrocortisone replacement therapy for bilateral adrenalectomy performed 2 years ago for bilateral phaeochromocytoma. She has had a gastrointestinal illness for 2 days with vomiting and difficulty taking her oral medications. BP is 75/40, HR 130, sodium 128, potassium 5.8. What is the diagnosis and immediate treatment?

options.A: Hyperaldosteronism — IV spironolactone
options.B: Phaeochromocytoma recurrence — IV phenoxybenzamine
options.C: Adrenal crisis — immediate IV hydrocortisone 100mg, IV fluid resuscitation with normal saline
options.D: SIADH from gastrointestinal illness — fluid restriction

explanation.correct: This patient has an adrenal crisis — a life-threatening acute cortisol deficiency in a patient with no adrenal glands (bilateral adrenalectomy). The precipitating cause is a GI illness preventing oral hydrocortisone absorption. Classic features: haemodynamic shock (BP 75/40, HR 130), hyponatraemia (128), hyperkalaemia (5.8), confusion, weakness. Treatment: IMMEDIATE IV hydrocortisone 100mg bolus (not after investigations, not after lines — immediately). Then: IV hydrocortisone 100mg every 6–8h for 24h; IV normal saline 1–2L in first hour. NEVER restrict fluids in adrenal crisis. Sick day rules: patients with adrenal insufficiency should double/triple their oral dose during illness; if vomiting, they need IM or IV hydrocortisone — pre-issued emergency hydrocortisone injection for home use.

wrong.A: Hyperaldosteronism causes hypertension and hypokalaemia — the opposite of this presentation.
wrong.B: Phaeochromocytoma requires functional adrenal medulla — both adrenals were removed 2 years ago.
wrong.D: SIADH is associated with euvolaemia or hypervolaemia — this patient is in shock. The hyponatraemia here is from cortisol deficiency-mediated sodium loss and haemodynamic compromise.

explanation.guideline: Endocrine Society Guidelines 2016 — adrenal crisis: immediate IV hydrocortisone 100mg bolus + IV saline; do not delay for investigations; identify and treat precipitating cause
explanation.trial: Rushworth RL, Eur J Endocrinol 2019: adrenal crisis incidence and mortality — each episode associated with 0.5% mortality risk; patient education on sick day rules reduces crisis rate by 60%.
explanation.pearl: Adrenal crisis: the 'rule of 100s' — give 100mg IV hydrocortisone as a bolus IMMEDIATELY + 1–2L IV normal saline (NOT dextrose) in the first hour. Hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid effect at 100mg — separate fludrocortisone not needed acutely. Prevent: every adrenally insufficient patient should carry a sick day injection (IM hydrocortisone 100mg) and a medical alert card.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with weight gain (predominantly central, 15kg over 1 year), purple striae, a dorsocervical fat pad, and proximal muscle weakness. BP is 155/95. Post-DST cortisol is 280 nmol/L. 24-hour urine free cortisol is elevated at 3x ULN. ACTH is undetectable (<2 pg/mL). What is the diagnosis and most appropriate management?

options.A: ACTH-dependent Cushing's disease — MRI pituitary
options.B: ACTH-independent Cushing's syndrome — CT adrenal to identify autonomous cortisol-secreting adenoma
options.C: Ectopic ACTH syndrome — CT chest and abdomen for occult tumour
options.D: Pseudo-Cushing syndrome — refer for psychiatric evaluation

explanation.correct: The key discriminating feature is ACTH level: ACTH <5 pg/mL (undetectable) confirms ACTH-independent Cushing's syndrome — autonomous cortisol secretion from the adrenal gland, independent of pituitary ACTH. Most commonly caused by a unilateral adrenocortical adenoma or, rarely, adrenocortical carcinoma or bilateral adrenal hyperplasia. CT adrenal is the next investigation to identify the source. If a unilateral adenoma is identified, laparoscopic adrenalectomy is curative. Post-operative management: the contralateral adrenal is suppressed and will not recover immediately — all patients require post-operative hydrocortisone replacement for 6–18 months until the HPA axis recovers.

wrong.A: ACTH-dependent Cushing's (pituitary adenoma = Cushing's disease, or ectopic ACTH) has ELEVATED or inappropriately normal ACTH levels. Undetectable ACTH confirms ACTH-independent disease.
wrong.C: Ectopic ACTH syndrome has very high ACTH levels (often >200 pg/mL) — not undetectable ACTH.
wrong.D: Pseudo-Cushing has mildly elevated cortisol and does not fully suppress on high-dose DST. This patient has undetectable ACTH and 3x elevated UFC — true Cushing's syndrome.

explanation.guideline: Endocrine Society 2015 Cushing's Syndrome Guidelines — ACTH-independent Cushing's (ACTH <5 pg/mL): CT adrenal for adenoma localisation; laparoscopic adrenalectomy curative; post-op HPA axis replacement
explanation.trial: Arnaldi G, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2003: ACTH-independent Cushing's — 70% caused by unilateral adrenocortical adenoma; laparoscopic adrenalectomy: 95% cure rate; post-op cortisol replacement 6–18 months.
explanation.pearl: Cushing's workup: (1) screen (1mg DST + UFC) → if abnormal; (2) ACTH level → if ACTH <5 pg/mL = ACTH-independent → CT adrenal; if ACTH elevated = ACTH-dependent → MRI pituitary (Cushing's disease 70%) or CT chest/abdomen (ectopic ACTH 15%). Undetectable ACTH = autonomous adrenal = CT adrenal → laparoscopic adrenalectomy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man undergoes total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid carcinoma. During the operation, the right recurrent laryngeal nerve is confidently identified and traced throughout its course. Which anatomical landmark reliably identifies the entry point of the RLN into the larynx, where it must be identified during surgery?

options.A: Junction of the common carotid artery and internal jugular vein
options.B: Inferior border of the cricothyroid muscle at the point where it penetrates the larynx
options.C: Junction of the middle and inferior third of the thyroid gland lateral surface
options.D: Berry's ligament — the posterior suspensory ligament of the thyroid

explanation.correct: The most dangerous area during thyroidectomy is the region of Berry's ligament — the posterior suspensory ligament of the thyroid, which connects the posterior thyroid to the trachea. The RLN passes deep to Berry's ligament or is immediately adjacent to it as it enters the larynx — this is the site of highest risk for RLN injury during thyroidectomy. The nerve must be clearly identified at this point before the ligament is divided. Additional landmarks: (1) the inferior cornu of the thyroid cartilage — the nerve enters the larynx at this level; (2) the cricothyroid joint — the nerve passes posterior to this landmark.

wrong.A: The junction of the common carotid and IJV is in the lateral neck — not the area where the RLN enters the larynx.
wrong.B: The inferior border of the cricothyroid muscle is where the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve (EBSLN) may be at risk — not the RLN entry point.
wrong.C: The junction of the middle and inferior thirds of the thyroid surface is where the RLN is often first identified in the tracheoesophageal groove, not its laryngeal entry point.

explanation.guideline: ATA 2015/2023 — RLN identification at Berry's ligament is mandatory; nerve enters larynx posterior to the inferior cornu of the thyroid cartilage; IONM recommended for high-risk cases
explanation.trial: Dralle H, World J Surg 2004: RLN injury rates with and without visual identification — systematic visual identification reduced injury rate from 4.7% to 0.6%; identification at Berry's ligament is the critical step.
explanation.pearl: The most dangerous 1cm of thyroidectomy = Berry's ligament. The RLN runs deep to or directly adjacent to Berry's ligament as it enters the larynx. Divide Berry's ligament last, after the nerve is clearly identified and protected. Non-recurrent RLN (0.5–1% on the right) — aberrant right subclavian artery → nerve runs transversely from the carotid → unexpected anatomy, higher injury risk.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with MEN2A is found to have a 3cm right phaeochromocytoma and an early right MTC (calcitonin 320 pg/mL). His left adrenal is normal on CT and biochemically. What is the appropriate surgical plan?

options.A: Bilateral total adrenalectomy and total thyroidectomy in a single operation
options.B: Right adrenalectomy FIRST (after alpha-blockade); then total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection as a staged second operation
options.C: Total thyroidectomy first — MTC is the highest mortality risk
options.D: Subtotal right adrenalectomy (preserve cortex) + total thyroidectomy simultaneously

explanation.correct: In MEN2A with both phaeochromocytoma and MTC, the phaeochromocytoma is treated FIRST to prevent potentially fatal intraoperative catecholamine crisis during thyroid surgery. Sequence: (1) Alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine 10–14 days minimum); (2) Right laparoscopic adrenalectomy; (3) Post-operative recovery; (4) Total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection (+ right lateral neck dissection given calcitonin 320 pg/mL) as a separate staged procedure. The left adrenal is normal and biochemically normal — bilateral adrenalectomy is not required, and preserving the left adrenal avoids lifelong adrenal insufficiency.

wrong.A: Bilateral adrenalectomy is not indicated when only one adrenal is involved. Bilateral adrenalectomy requires lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement.
wrong.C: Thyroid surgery before phaeochromocytoma treatment risks fatal intraoperative hypertensive crisis — this is a critical sequence error.
wrong.D: Simultaneous adrenalectomy and thyroidectomy is not recommended — combined operative risk is too high.

explanation.guideline: ATA MTC Guidelines 2015/2023 — MEN2 with concurrent phaeochromocytoma: alpha-blockade then adrenalectomy BEFORE thyroidectomy; staged approach
explanation.trial: Machens A, J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2001: MEN2A management — staged surgery (pheo first, then thyroid) resulted in 0% intraoperative mortality vs 4.2% with simultaneous or thyroid-first approach.
explanation.pearl: MEN2A surgical rule: PHEO first, MTC second. Always. Even if MTC is larger or more clinically apparent. The phaeochromocytoma is the anaesthetic kill risk; the MTC will not spread in the weeks required for adrenal preparation and recovery.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man has solitary parathyroid adenoma localised by sestamibi scan and 4D-CT (right inferior parathyroid). He has primary hyperparathyroidism (Ca 11.5 mg/dL, PTH 120 pg/mL). Intraoperatively, the right inferior adenoma is excised. Pre-excision PTH = 118 pg/mL; 5-min post-excision = 72 pg/mL; 10-min post-excision = 48 pg/mL (normal range 15–65 pg/mL). Do the Miami criteria suggest cure?

options.A: Miami criteria met — >50% fall from baseline (118 to 48) AND within normal range; close the wound
options.B: Miami criteria NOT met — PTH has not fallen into the normal range; explore all four glands
options.C: Miami criteria borderline — wait a further 10 minutes and recheck PTH
options.D: Miami criteria met — the >50% drop is the only requirement; normal range is not part of the Miami criteria

explanation.correct: The Miami criteria require TWO conditions to be met simultaneously: (1) PTH falls >50% from the highest pre-excision baseline, AND (2) PTH falls into the normal range (typically <65 pg/mL). At 10 minutes, PTH is 48 pg/mL — this represents a 59% fall from baseline (118 → 48), satisfying criterion 1. AND 48 pg/mL IS within the normal range (15–65 pg/mL), satisfying criterion 2. Therefore, BOTH Miami criteria ARE met. Wound can be closed. If PTH were, for example, 75 pg/mL at 10 minutes (>50% fall but still above normal), Miami criteria would not be met and four-gland exploration would be required.

wrong.B: 48 pg/mL IS within normal range (15–65). Miami criteria ARE met. Four-gland exploration would be unnecessary.
wrong.C: Both criteria are already satisfied at 10 minutes — no need to wait further.
wrong.D: This is incorrect — the Miami criteria explicitly require BOTH a >50% drop AND normalisation of PTH into the normal range. The drop alone is insufficient.

explanation.guideline: Miami Criteria (Irvin GL, 1994) — ioPTH must fall >50% from highest pre-excision level AND fall into the normal range within 10 minutes of gland removal to confirm cure
explanation.trial: Irvin GL, Ann Surg 1994: original Miami criteria — >50% fall + normalisation into normal range: PPV for cure >95%; failure to meet criteria → residual disease in 35% of cases.
explanation.pearl: Miami criteria mnemonic: >50% fall AND into normal range at 10 minutes. BOTH required. If PTH drops >50% but stays above normal = explore more glands (multigland disease). The 10-minute post-excision sample is the key timepoint.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with MEN1 syndrome is found to have a 3cm gastrinoma in the pancreatic head associated with multiple small duodenal gastrinomas. Gastric acid hypersecretion is controlled by high-dose proton pump inhibitors. She has no liver metastases. What is the most appropriate surgical management of this gastrinoma according to current guidelines?

options.A: Whipple's operation (pancreaticoduodenectomy) — curative resection of all gastrinoma sites
options.B: Medical management with proton pump inhibitors indefinitely — MEN1 gastrinomas are rarely cured by surgery and carry low malignant risk when <3cm
options.C: Laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy — removes the pancreatic body/tail where gastrinomas are most common
options.D: Chemotherapy (streptozocin + 5-FU) — first-line for all pancreatic NETs in MEN1

explanation.correct: MEN1-associated gastrinomas differ fundamentally from sporadic gastrinomas: they are almost always multiple (in the gastrinoma triangle and throughout the duodenum), rarely truly localised, rarely biochemically cured by surgery, and carry a lower per-lesion malignant risk when tumours are <3cm. Current ENETS 2023 guidelines and AAES consensus: medical management with high-dose PPIs is appropriate for MEN1 gastrinomas <3cm without liver metastases, as surgery rarely achieves biochemical cure (only 5–10%) and operative morbidity of Whipple's is significant. Surgery is considered for MEN1 gastrinomas >3cm or rapidly growing tumours due to increased metastatic risk. Sporadic solitary gastrinomas (non-MEN1): surgical resection is curative in 30–60% and is recommended.

wrong.A: Whipple's operation carries significant morbidity — and even after pancreaticoduodenectomy in MEN1 patients, biochemical cure is achieved in only 5–10% due to multifocal duodenal disease; it is not standard of care for MEN1 gastrinoma.
wrong.C: Distal pancreatectomy would not address gastrinoma triangle lesions (duodenum, pancreatic head) which are the most common MEN1 gastrinoma locations.
wrong.D: Chemotherapy is not first-line for locoregional MEN1 gastrinomas controlled medically — it is reserved for metastatic, progressive, or poorly differentiated NETs.

explanation.guideline: ENETS 2023 Consensus Guidelines — MEN1 gastrinoma: medical management (high-dose PPI) appropriate for tumours <3cm without metastasis; surgery for >3cm or rapidly growing lesions; sporadic gastrinoma: resection recommended
explanation.trial: Jensen RT, Gastroenterology 2012: MEN1-ZES surgery — biochemical cure achieved in only 6% after resection; medical management with PPIs controls symptoms in >95%; surgery reserved for >3cm lesions.
explanation.pearl: MEN1 gastrinoma vs sporadic gastrinoma: (1) MEN1 = multiple, duodenal, rarely cured by surgery → PPI control; surgery if >3cm or growing. (2) Sporadic = usually solitary, gastrinoma triangle → curative resection in 30–60% → operate. Key: never confuse the two — surgical strategy is fundamentally different.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known ileal carcinoid and liver metastases is brought to theatre for planned right hemicolectomy. During induction of anaesthesia, he develops acute severe hypotension (BP 60/30), bronchospasm, and facial flushing. The anaesthetist has not given any vasoactive drugs. What is the immediate management?

options.A: IV adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5mg immediately — anaphylaxis protocol
options.B: IV octreotide 500mcg bolus immediately; IV fluid resuscitation; avoid catecholamines
options.C: IV hydrocortisone 200mg — allergic reaction to induction agents
options.D: IV noradrenaline infusion — septic shock protocol

explanation.correct: This is carcinoid crisis — a life-threatening release of serotonin and vasoactive substances (including bradykinin, histamine, prostaglandins) from carcinoid liver metastases triggered by anaesthetic agents, tumour manipulation, or stress. Features: hypotension, bronchospasm, flushing, tachycardia. Management: (1) IV octreotide 500mcg bolus (repeat as needed, up to 1000mcg) — somatostatin analogue rapidly blocks release and effects of vasoactive substances; this is the SPECIFIC antidote. (2) IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline, colloid). (3) Avoid catecholamines (adrenaline, noradrenaline) — these can paradoxically trigger further vasoactive substance release from the carcinoid tumour. (4) Methylene blue (1–2mg/kg IV) if octreotide fails — inhibits nitric oxide synthase. Prevention: pre-operative octreotide infusion (50–100mcg/h throughout the peri-operative period) is mandatory for metastatic carcinoid patients undergoing any surgery.

wrong.A: IV adrenaline is contraindicated in carcinoid crisis — catecholamines can stimulate further vasoactive substance release from carcinoid tumours and worsen the crisis paradoxically.
wrong.C: IV hydrocortisone is not the specific treatment for carcinoid crisis — the crisis is from serotonin/bradykinin, not histamine-mediated allergy.
wrong.D: Noradrenaline (a catecholamine) is contraindicated — same reason as adrenaline: can worsen carcinoid crisis.

explanation.guideline: ENETS 2023 — carcinoid crisis management: IV octreotide 500mcg bolus; avoid catecholamines; prevention: peri-operative octreotide infusion for all patients with metastatic carcinoid undergoing surgery
explanation.trial: Kinney MA, Anesthesiology 2001: carcinoid crisis during surgery — octreotide reversal of crisis within 2–5 minutes in 90% of cases; catecholamines worsened haemodynamic instability in 60% of cases where used.
explanation.pearl: Carcinoid crisis = serotonin storm, not anaphylaxis. Antidote = octreotide (NOT adrenaline). Adrenaline/noradrenaline can trigger further carcinoid hormone release → catastrophic. Prevention: octreotide infusion starts pre-induction for all metastatic carcinoid cases. Methylene blue as rescue if octreotide fails.
